student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Reading the Standard -- Interpreting an International Signaling Specification
lesson.menu_desc: A German signaling supplier sends the interface specification for the Line 4 interlocking, and before you sign a conformance statement you have to read it the way the standard means it -- not the way it looks. Tonight you learn the grammar that carries a specification: shall for a mandatory requirement, should for a recommendation, may for a permission, must not for a prohibition. You meet the words of a standard (clause, conformance, deviation permit, applicable standard, scope of application, verification method, to deem, provided that, notwithstanding), you read a real extract of Clause 7 the way the supplier's engineer reads it, and you get on a clarification call where you separate what the interlocking shall do from what it should do, confirm conformance, and decide whether a change needs a deviation permit -- 40 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 18 -- Reading the Standard
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 18 -- Reading the Standard
lesson.grammar_point: shall / should / may / must (and shall not / must not) for obligation, recommendation, permission and prohibition in technical specifications

listenings.text: João, before you get on the clarification call with the German supplier, let me tell you how I want you to read this specification, because the whole conformance statement hangs on reading it correctly. The document looks like plain English, and that is the trap. In a standard, the modal verbs are not style, they are the contract. When a clause says the interlocking shall fail to a safe state, shall means mandatory. It is not a prediction about the future, the way will would be, and it is not a polite suggestion. It is a requirement you have to meet and demonstrate, or you cannot claim conformance. So every shall in that document is a line item you own. Now, should is the one people get wrong in both directions. Should is a recommendation, strongly advised good practice, but it is not binding. A supplier who departs from a should does not lose conformance, provided that the departure is recorded and justified. Do not read a should as if it were a shall and over-specify the contract, and do not read it as optional, therefore ignore it, because the intent still matters. Then there is may. May is permission, not obligation. When the clause says the contractor may propose an equivalent component, it means you are allowed to, not that you have to. And must, when you see it, is a necessity, usually a safety one. Must not is an absolute prohibition, full stop. So as you read, mark every clause: is this a shall, a should, a may, or a must not. Get that map right and the call is easy. Confirm the mandatory requirements, flag the recommendations, and if a change touches a shall, tell them a deviation permit is needed before any work proceeds.

Three rules for reading a specification, from someone who has audited a lot of conformance claims. First, the modal verb is the requirement, so read it before you read anything else. Engineers skim a clause for the technical noun and miss whether it says shall or should, and those two words decide whether the clause is binding. Underline the modal in every clause before you argue about the content. Shall and shall not are mandatory. Should and should not are recommended. May is permitted. Must and must not carry necessity and prohibition. If you cannot say which of those a clause is, you have not read it yet. Second, respect the scope of application. A requirement only binds inside the scope the clause names, and a cross-reference tells you which other clause you have to read with it. People quote a shall from a section that does not even apply to their equipment, or they read one clause alone when it points to an annex that changes it. Find the scope, follow the cross-reference, and only then decide what conforms. Third, know the two honest ways out of a requirement you cannot meet. Either the product is deemed to conform because it passed the stated verification method, the type test, the inspection, or the analysis the standard names, or, if it genuinely cannot meet a mandatory clause, you apply for a deviation permit and you do not proceed on that function until the permit is approved in writing. Conformance is not an opinion and it is not a negotiation. It is every shall met and demonstrated, every deviation documented, and nothing on the vital core changed without authorization. Read the modal, respect the scope, and prove it by the method the standard sets. That is the whole discipline.

patterns.title: Shall / should / may / must -- how a specification sets its terms
cards: shall / shall not = a MANDATORY requirement

In a standard, shall is not the future and not a suggestion -- it is binding. "The interlocking shall fail to a safe state." To claim conformance, every shall must be met and demonstrated. Shall not is the mandatory prohibition: "Work shall not proceed until the permit is approved."

Should is good practice you are advised to follow, but a departure does not break conformance, provided that it is recorded and justified. "The supplier should provide the records in English." Reading a should as a shall over-specifies the contract; reading it as "optional, so ignore it" misses the intent.
may = a PERMISSION, not a duty

May grants an option, never an obligation. "The contractor may apply for a deviation permit." It means you are permitted to, not that you have to. Do not confuse may (permission) with might (possibility): may as an obligation, or as a mere maybe, both misread the clause.
must / must not = NECESSITY / absolute prohibition

Must marks a necessity, usually safety: "The safety case must be approved before energization." Must not is an absolute prohibition that no recommendation can soften: "Access to the vital core must not be granted without dual authorization."

patterns.text: One habit runs through all of it: read the modal before you read the content. The technical noun tells you what the clause is about; the modal -- shall, should, may, must not -- tells you whether it binds. Every shall is a line item you have to meet and prove by the verification method the standard names; every should is advice you may depart from with justification; every may is a door you can open or leave shut; every must not is a wall. Skim the noun and miss the modal, and you sign a conformance statement you cannot actually stand behind.

reading.rtitle: Interlocking Interface Specification — Extract, Clause 7: Conformance, Recommendations and Deviations (adapted, written for João's package)
paras: 7.1 Scope of application. This clause applies to the vital interlocking core and its interface to the trackside objects; it does not cover the operator workstation, which is governed by a separate applicable standard. Within this scope of application, every requirement written with shall is a mandatory requirement. The interlocking shall fail to a safe state on loss of power, and it shall log every command with a time stamp. A product is deemed to conform to this clause only when each shall has been met and demonstrated by the stated verification method — type test, inspection, or analysis.

7.2 Recommendations. Requirements written with should are optional requirements: strongly recommended good practice, but not binding for conformance. The supplier should provide the test records in English, and the diagnostic messages should follow the naming convention in Annex B — see the cross-reference in 7.2.3. A supplier who departs from a should does not lose conformance, provided that the departure is recorded and justified in the safety case. Reading a should as if it were a shall over-specifies the contract; reading it as ‘optional, therefore ignore it’ misses the intent.

7.3 Permissions and prohibitions. The contractor may propose an equivalent component, provided that it holds the same safety integrity level; this is a permission, not a duty. Where a mandatory requirement cannot be met, the contractor may apply for a deviation permit, and work shall not proceed on the affected function until that permit is approved in writing. One prohibition is absolute: access to the vital core must not be granted without dual authorization. Notwithstanding 7.3, the safety case must be approved before energization — a must here signals a necessity that no recommendation can soften.
reading.source: Motiva Signaling -- interface specification extract, Line 4 (fictional, written for João's package)

reading.text: Read Clause 7 by its modals and the whole extract organizes itself. 7.1 is all shall: mandatory, demonstrated by a verification method, or no conformance. 7.2 is all should: recommended, and a departure is allowed provided that it is justified. 7.3 is may and must not: a permission to propose or to apply for a deviation permit, and an absolute prohibition on the vital core. The nouns change -- fail-safe, time stamp, naming convention, integrity level -- but the force of each line is set by one word. That is exactly the map you carry into the call: which lines are shall, which are should, and where a change needs a deviation permit before anyone proceeds.

choices: Listing the daily maintenance tasks for the trackside signaling cabinets on Line 4.

Defining how to read the specification's own language -- which requirements are mandatory (shall), which are recommended (should), and where the contractor is permitted (may) or forbidden (must not) -- and how conformance and deviations work.
Cancelling the interface specification and replacing it with a different applicable standard.
Setting the commercial price and delivery schedule for the interlocking units.

items: Under Clause 7.1, every requirement written with "shall" is mandatory, and a product only conforms when each one is met and demonstrated.
t

"...every requirement written with shall is a mandatory requirement... A product is deemed to conform to this clause only when each shall has been met and demonstrated by the stated verification method." Shall is the binding line.
A supplier who departs from a "should" automatically loses conformance with the clause.
f

The opposite: "Requirements written with should are optional requirements... not binding for conformance. A supplier who departs from a should does not lose conformance, provided that the departure is recorded and justified."
The contractor is obliged to propose an equivalent component for the interface.
f
"The contractor may propose an equivalent component, provided that it holds the same safety integrity level; this is a permission, not a duty." May grants an option, not an obligation.
Work may continue on the affected function while a deviation permit is still waiting for written approval.
f
"...work shall not proceed on the affected function until that permit is approved in writing." Shall not is a mandatory prohibition until the permit is signed.
Notwithstanding clause 7.3, the safety case must be approved before energization.
t
"Notwithstanding 7.3, the safety case must be approved before energization -- a must here signals a necessity that no recommendation can soften." Notwithstanding overrides the named clause.

items.situation: A clause reads: "The interlocking shall fail to a safe state on loss of power." Anja asks if that line is negotiable. Answer, using the meaning of shall.
tips: No -- shall makes it a mandatory requirement. To claim conformance, the interlocking shall fail safe, and we have to demonstrate it by the stated verification method.
That one isn't negotiable: shall means binding. It's not future and it's not advice -- it's a line item we have to meet and prove.

items.situation: The spec says: "The supplier should provide the test records in English." Anja can only deliver them in German this week. Tell her what that means for conformance.
tips: Should is a recommendation, not a shall, so you don't lose conformance -- provided that the departure is recorded and justified in the safety case.
That's a should, so it's advised, not binding. Send them in German for now, log the deviation and the reason, and we're still conformant.

items.situation: Anja wants to swap in an equivalent axle counter. State what the spec permits, using may.
tips: You may propose an equivalent component, provided that it holds the same safety integrity level -- it's a permission, so it's your option, not an obligation.
The contractor may substitute it, provided that the SIL is the same. If you'd rather keep the original, that's fine too -- may is a choice.

items.situation: A mandatory requirement genuinely cannot be met on one function. Tell Anja the correct route and the limit on work, using "may" and "shall not".
tips: You may apply for a deviation permit -- but work shall not proceed on that function until the permit is approved in writing.
The honest way out is a deviation permit; apply for it, and until it's signed, work shall not go ahead on the affected function.

items.situation: Anja asks whether her team can get into the vital core to speed up a test. Give the prohibition, using "must not".
tips: Access to the vital core must not be granted without dual authorization -- that's an absolute prohibition, no exceptions for schedule.
I'm sorry, but must not is a wall here: no access to the vital core without dual authorization, whatever the pressure on time.

items.situation: You need to confirm what makes the product officially conformant. State the rule, using "deemed" and "verification method".
tips: The product is deemed to conform only when every shall is met and demonstrated by the stated verification method -- the type test, the inspection, or the analysis.
Conformance isn't an opinion: it's deemed once each mandatory requirement is proven by the verification method the standard names.

bank.label: Naming the force of a clause
items: That line uses shall, so it's a mandatory requirement -- binding for conformance, not a suggestion.
This one is a should: it's recommended good practice, but a justified departure doesn't break conformance.
That's a may -- a permission, not a duty. You're allowed to, but you don't have to.

bank.label: Scope, cross-reference and applicability
items: Before we apply that shall, let's check the scope of application -- it may not cover this part of the interface.
Clause 7.2 has a cross-reference to Annex B; we have to read them together before we decide what conforms.
The operator workstation is outside this scope -- it's governed by a separate applicable standard.

bank.label: Conformance and verification
items: The product is deemed to conform only when every shall is met and demonstrated by the stated verification method.
For each mandatory requirement, the standard names the verification method -- type test, inspection, or analysis.
Conformance isn't a negotiation: it's every shall proven and every deviation documented.

bank.label: Deviations and prohibitions
items: If we can't meet that mandatory requirement, we may apply for a deviation permit, provided that safety is preserved.
Work shall not proceed on the affected function until the deviation permit is approved in writing.
Notwithstanding that clause, access to the vital core must not be granted without dual authorization.
